name.text: Midterm 2 - Q2V1: RLC Transient + Debug (Series RLC Natural Response, Underdamped)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q2V1: RLC Transient with Waveform Debug (Series RLC Natural Response, Underdamped)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 9 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.8.

Circuit Description

A series RLC circuit is powered by a DC voltage source \(V_s\). The capacitor is fully charged in steady state. At \(t = 0\), a switch disconnects the source, leaving the series R-L-C loop closed for current to flow.

Component Values

  • \(R = 10\;\Omega\)
  • \(L = 10\;\text{mH}\)
  • \(C = 50\;\mu\text{F}\)
  • Source: \(V_s = 20\;\text{V}\)

Initial Conditions

Before switching: \(v_C(0^-) = V_s\), \(i_L(0^-) = 0\) (capacitor fully charged, no current in steady state).

Part (a) — Damping Parameters and Classification (2 points)

Calculate the neper frequency (damping coefficient) \(\alpha\) and the resonant frequency \(\omega_0\).

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans1]] \(\text{Np/s}\)   [[validation:ans1]]

Enter a number, e.g. 1234

\(\omega_0 = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{rad/s}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 5678

Classify the damping regime: [[input:ans3]]   [[validation:ans3]]

Part (b) — Response Value (2 points)

Calculate the current \(i(t_1)\) at \(t_1 = 1\;\text{ms}\).

\(i(t_1) = \) [[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (c) — Waveform Error Identification (2 points)

A student attempted to plot the response and made errors. Their waveform is described below:

A student plotted \(i(t)\) for \(t > 0\). Their plot shows:
(i) The current starts at \(i(0^+) = 2\;\text{A}\) (but it should start at 0).
(ii) The oscillation envelope decays with a time constant of 4 ms (but \(1/\alpha = 2\;\text{ms}\)).
(iii) The oscillation frequency appears correct.
(iv) The current approaches 0 as \(t \to \infty\) (correct for natural response).

Select ALL statements that identify actual errors in the student’s waveform:

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

Part (d) — Parameter Sensitivity (3 points, manually graded)

Suppose the resistance \(R\) is doubled.

  1. How does this change \(\alpha\)?
  2. Does the damping regime change? If so, from what to what?
  3. Sketch the qualitative shape of the new response and explain whether energy dissipation is faster or slower.

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V1 (Series RLC Natural Response, Underdamped)

Part (a):

\(\\alpha = \\frac{R}{2L} = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/s}\)

\(\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = {@ta_omega0@}\;\text{rad/s}\)

Since \(\alpha < \omega_0\), the circuit is underdamped.

Part (b): \(i(t_1) = {@ta_value@}\)

Part (c): The errors are statements {@correct_errors@}.

Part (d): When R doubles:

  • \(\alpha\) doubles (since \(\alpha = R/(2L)\))
  • The damping regime may change depending on the new \(\alpha\) vs \(\omega_0\)
  • More damping = faster energy dissipation, response decays quicker

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: You used the wrong formula. Check whether this is a series or parallel RLC circuit and use the correct \(\alpha\) formula.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect \(\alpha\). Review the damping coefficient formula for this circuit topology.
Incorrect. \(\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}\).
Incorrect. Compare \(\alpha\) with \(\omega_0\): if \(\alpha < \omega_0\), underdamped; if \(\alpha > \omega_0\), overdamped; if \(\alpha = \omega_0\), critically damped.
Incorrect. Review the response formula for this damping regime and check your calculations.
Not all waveform errors were correctly identified.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q2V2: RLC Transient + Debug (Parallel RLC Step Response, Overdamped)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q2V2: RLC Transient with Waveform Debug (Parallel RLC Step Response, Overdamped)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 9 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.8.

Circuit Description

A parallel RLC circuit has zero initial conditions. At \(t = 0\), a DC current source \(I_s\) is switched on, delivering current to the parallel combination of R, L, and C.

Component Values

  • \(R = 20\;\Omega\)
  • \(L = 100\;\text{mH}\)
  • \(C = 10\;\mu\text{F}\)
  • Source: \(I_s = 100\;\text{mA}\)

Initial Conditions

Before switching: \(v(0^-) = 0\), \(i_L(0^-) = 0\) (all zero initial conditions).

Part (a) — Damping Parameters and Classification (2 points)

Calculate the neper frequency (damping coefficient) \(\alpha\) and the resonant frequency \(\omega_0\).

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans1]] \(\text{Np/s}\)   [[validation:ans1]]

Enter a number, e.g. 1234

\(\omega_0 = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{rad/s}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 5678

Classify the damping regime: [[input:ans3]]   [[validation:ans3]]

Part (b) — Response Value (2 points)

Calculate the voltage \(v(t_1)\) across the parallel combination at \(t_1 = 0.5\;\text{ms}\).

\(v(t_1) = \) [[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (c) — Waveform Error Identification (2 points)

A student attempted to plot the response and made errors. Their waveform is described below:

A student plotted \(v(t)\) for \(t > 0\). Their plot shows:
(i) The voltage starts at \(v(0^+) = 0\) (correct).
(ii) The voltage oscillates with a damped sinusoid (but the circuit is overdamped!).
(iii) The voltage approaches \(v(\infty) = I_s \cdot R = 2\;\text{V}\) (but at DC, the inductor is a short, so \(v(\infty) = 0\)).
(iv) The voltage rises then falls back — double exponential shape.

Select ALL statements that identify actual errors in the student’s waveform:

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

Part (d) — Parameter Sensitivity (3 points, manually graded)

Suppose the resistance \(R\) is doubled.

  1. How does this change \(\alpha\)?
  2. Does the damping regime change? If so, from what to what?
  3. Sketch the qualitative shape of the new response and explain whether energy dissipation is faster or slower.

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V2 (Parallel RLC Step Response, Overdamped)

Part (a):

\(\\alpha = \\frac{1}{2RC} = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/s}\)

\(\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = {@ta_omega0@}\;\text{rad/s}\)

Since \(\alpha > \omega_0\), the circuit is overdamped.

Part (b): \(v(t_1) = {@ta_value@}\)

Part (c): The errors are statements {@correct_errors@}.

Part (d): When R doubles:

  • \(\alpha\) halves (since \(\alpha = 1/(2RC)\))
  • The damping regime may change depending on the new \(\alpha\) vs \(\omega_0\)
  • Less damping = slower energy dissipation, response oscillates more

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: You used the wrong formula. Check whether this is a series or parallel RLC circuit and use the correct \(\alpha\) formula.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect \(\alpha\). Review the damping coefficient formula for this circuit topology.
Incorrect. \(\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}\).
Incorrect. Compare \(\alpha\) with \(\omega_0\): if \(\alpha < \omega_0\), underdamped; if \(\alpha > \omega_0\), overdamped; if \(\alpha = \omega_0\), critically damped.
Incorrect. Review the response formula for this damping regime and check your calculations.
Not all waveform errors were correctly identified.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q2V3: RLC Transient + Debug (Series RLC Step Response, Critically Damped)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q2V3: RLC Transient with Waveform Debug (Series RLC Step Response, Critically Damped)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 9 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.8.

Circuit Description

A series RLC circuit with zero initial conditions is connected to a DC voltage source \(V_s\) at \(t = 0\). The circuit response is the capacitor voltage \(v_C(t)\).

Component Values

  • \(R = 200\;\Omega\)
  • \(L = 100\;\text{mH}\)
  • \(C = 10\;\mu\text{F}\)
  • Source: \(V_s = 10\;\text{V}\)

Initial Conditions

Before switching: \(v_C(0^-) = 0\), \(i_L(0^-) = 0\) (zero initial conditions).

Part (a) — Damping Parameters and Classification (2 points)

Calculate the neper frequency (damping coefficient) \(\alpha\) and the resonant frequency \(\omega_0\).

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans1]] \(\text{Np/s}\)   [[validation:ans1]]

Enter a number, e.g. 1234

\(\omega_0 = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{rad/s}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 5678

Classify the damping regime: [[input:ans3]]   [[validation:ans3]]

Part (b) — Response Value (2 points)

Calculate the capacitor voltage \(v_C(t_1)\) at \(t_1 = 2\;\text{ms}\).

\(v_C(t_1) = \) [[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (c) — Waveform Error Identification (2 points)

A student attempted to plot the response and made errors. Their waveform is described below:

A student plotted \(v_C(t)\) for \(t > 0\). Their plot shows:
(i) \(v_C(0^+) = V_s = 10\;\text{V}\) (but it should start at 0!).
(ii) The voltage overshoots \(V_s\) before settling (but critically damped response does NOT overshoot!).
(iii) The final value is \(v_C(\infty) = V_s = 10\;\text{V}\) (correct).
(iv) The response reaches 63% of final value in about 1 ms.

Select ALL statements that identify actual errors in the student’s waveform:

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

Part (d) — Parameter Sensitivity (3 points, manually graded)

Suppose the resistance \(R\) is doubled.

  1. How does this change \(\alpha\)?
  2. Does the damping regime change? If so, from what to what?
  3. Sketch the qualitative shape of the new response and explain whether energy dissipation is faster or slower.

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V3 (Series RLC Step Response, Critically Damped)

Part (a):

\(\\alpha = \\frac{R}{2L} = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/s}\)

\(\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = {@ta_omega0@}\;\text{rad/s}\)

Since \(\alpha = \omega_0\), the circuit is critically damped.

Part (b): \(v_C(t_1) = {@ta_value@}\)

Part (c): The errors are statements {@correct_errors@}.

Part (d): When R doubles:

  • \(\alpha\) doubles (since \(\alpha = R/(2L)\))
  • The damping regime may change depending on the new \(\alpha\) vs \(\omega_0\)
  • More damping = faster energy dissipation, response decays quicker

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: You used the wrong formula. Check whether this is a series or parallel RLC circuit and use the correct \(\alpha\) formula.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect \(\alpha\). Review the damping coefficient formula for this circuit topology.
Incorrect. \(\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}\).
Incorrect. Compare \(\alpha\) with \(\omega_0\): if \(\alpha < \omega_0\), underdamped; if \(\alpha > \omega_0\), overdamped; if \(\alpha = \omega_0\), critically damped.
Incorrect. Review the response formula for this damping regime and check your calculations.
Not all waveform errors were correctly identified.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q2V4: RLC Transient + Debug (Parallel RLC Natural Response, Underdamped)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q2V4: RLC Transient with Waveform Debug (Parallel RLC Natural Response, Underdamped)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 9 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Nilsson & Riedel Ch.8.

Circuit Description

A parallel RLC circuit has a capacitor pre-charged to \(V_0\). At \(t = 0\), a switch closes, connecting the capacitor to R and L in parallel. The voltage \(v(t)\) across the combination is the response.

Component Values

  • \(R = 100\;\Omega\)
  • \(L = 25\;\text{mH}\)
  • \(C = 5\;\mu\text{F}\)
  • Source: \(V_0 = 15\;\text{V}\)

Initial Conditions

Before switching: \(v(0^-) = V_0 = 15\;\text{V}\), \(i_L(0^-) = 0\).

Part (a) — Damping Parameters and Classification (2 points)

Calculate the neper frequency (damping coefficient) \(\alpha\) and the resonant frequency \(\omega_0\).

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans1]] \(\text{Np/s}\)   [[validation:ans1]]

Enter a number, e.g. 1234

\(\omega_0 = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{rad/s}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 5678

Classify the damping regime: [[input:ans3]]   [[validation:ans3]]

Part (b) — Response Value (2 points)

Calculate the voltage \(v(t_1)\) at \(t_1 = 0.5\;\text{ms}\).

\(v(t_1) = \) [[input:ans4]]   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 0.567

Part (c) — Waveform Error Identification (2 points)

A student attempted to plot the response and made errors. Their waveform is described below:

A student plotted \(v(t)\) for \(t > 0\). Their plot shows:
(i) The voltage starts at \(v(0^+) = V_0 = 15\;\text{V}\) (correct).
(ii) The envelope decays with time constant \(1/\omega_0\) instead of \(1/\alpha\).
(iii) The oscillation frequency is \(\omega_0\) instead of the damped frequency \(\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}\).
(iv) The voltage approaches 0 as \(t \to \infty\) (correct for natural response).

Select ALL statements that identify actual errors in the student’s waveform:

[[input:ans5]]   [[validation:ans5]]

Part (d) — Parameter Sensitivity (3 points, manually graded)

Suppose the resistance \(R\) is doubled.

  1. How does this change \(\alpha\)?
  2. Does the damping regime change? If so, from what to what?
  3. Sketch the qualitative shape of the new response and explain whether energy dissipation is faster or slower.

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V4 (Parallel RLC Natural Response, Underdamped)

Part (a):

\(\\alpha = \\frac{1}{2RC} = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/s}\)

\(\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = {@ta_omega0@}\;\text{rad/s}\)

Since \(\alpha < \omega_0\), the circuit is underdamped.

Part (b): \(v(t_1) = {@ta_value@}\)

Part (c): The errors are statements {@correct_errors@}.

Part (d): When R doubles:

  • \(\alpha\) halves (since \(\alpha = 1/(2RC)\))
  • The damping regime may change depending on the new \(\alpha\) vs \(\omega_0\)
  • Less damping = slower energy dissipation, response oscillates more

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: You used the wrong formula. Check whether this is a series or parallel RLC circuit and use the correct \(\alpha\) formula.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect \(\alpha\). Review the damping coefficient formula for this circuit topology.
Incorrect. \(\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}\).
Incorrect. Compare \(\alpha\) with \(\omega_0\): if \(\alpha < \omega_0\), underdamped; if \(\alpha > \omega_0\), overdamped; if \(\alpha = \omega_0\), critically damped.
Incorrect. Review the response formula for this damping regime and check your calculations.
Not all waveform errors were correctly identified.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q2: Upload Handwritten Work (RLC Transient)

questiontext.text:
Upload Your Handwritten Work for Q2

Upload clear photos or PDF scans of your handwritten work for Question 2 (RLC Transient with Waveform Debug).

Your uploads must include:

  • Part (a):
    • \(\alpha\) calculation with the correct formula for the circuit topology
    • \(\omega_0\) calculation
    • Classification logic: comparison of \(\alpha\) vs \(\omega_0\)
  • Part (b):
    • Response formula setup (which form: overdamped, underdamped, or critically damped)
    • Coefficient calculation from initial conditions
    • Evaluation at \(t_1\)
  • Part (d):
    • New \(\alpha\) calculation with doubled R
    • Qualitative waveform sketch showing the change in behavior
    • Energy dissipation reasoning

Upload Instructions:

  1. Write your name and student ID on every page.
  2. Use a dark pen (blue or black) on white paper.
  3. Ensure photos are legible.
  4. Up to 2 files. Accepted: PDF, JPG, JPEG, PNG.

Worth 0 points. Supports grading verification.

Teacher notice: Set “Allow attachments” to 2+ in quiz settings.

generalfeedback.text: Thank you for uploading your handwritten work for Q2.

graderinfo.text:
Instructor Notes — Q2 Handwritten Work Review

  • Check correct \(\alpha\) formula for the topology (series vs parallel)
  • Verify response form matches the damping regime
  • For Part (d): look for correct reasoning about how R affects \(\alpha\) and the regime
  • Red flags: Answers without supporting calculations, inconsistent handwriting
